title: Foreign aid given for emergencies and longer-term development
subtitle: Committed bilateral [official development assistance (ODA)](#dod:oda) from governments and multilateral organizations, grants from civil society organizations. Aid for emergencies, such as natural disasters and wars, is often called humanitarian aid. This data is expressed in US dollars and adjusted for inflation.
caption:
Foreign aid given for emergencies and longer-term development

Committed bilateral official development assistance (ODA) from governments and multilateral organizations, grants from civil society organizations. Aid for emergencies, such as natural disasters and wars, is often called humanitarian aid. This data is expressed in US dollars and adjusted for inflation.

This stacked area chart tracks foreign aid flows from 1971 to 2024, with values expressed in constant 2023 US dollars. The vertical axis ranges from $0 to $200 billion, marked in increments of $20 billion. The chart distinguishes between two categories: "Aid for emergencies," represented by a pink area at the bottom, and "Aid for longer-term development," represented by a green area stacked on top. From 1971 through the late 1990s, the total aid volume generally fluctuates between $40 billion and $70 billion, with a distinct spike reaching nearly $100 billion around 1991. During this early period, the pink area representing emergency aid remains a thin band near the baseline, rarely exceeding $5 billion. Starting in the early 2000s, the total volume begins a sustained upward trend, crossing the $100 billion mark around 2005 and reaching approximately $140 billion by 2015. The pink emergency aid band widens gradually during this time, occupying a larger share of the total height. The most significant growth occurs after 2020, where the total height surges to a peak just above $180 billion around 2023 before declining slightly in 2024. At this peak, the green development aid section constitutes the majority of the height, extending from roughly $30 billion to over $180 billion, while the pink emergency aid section sits between the baseline and the $30 billion mark.
